rows.v: The overall identity, reputation, and emotional associations a company holds in customers' minds — far more than just a logo. What people feel when they hear the name.
Years of consistent messaging built a brand so trusted that customers paid a premium without blinking.
From Old Norse 'brandr' (to burn) — ranchers literally branded cattle to mark ownership. The mark of identity evolved into the modern business meaning.
The distinct place a brand occupies in customers' minds relative to competitors. How you want to be perceived — premium, budget, rebellious, reliable.
They sharpened their positioning as the affordable alternative, owning the value-conscious shopper's mind.
Popularized by the 1981 book 'Positioning' by Ries and Trout, who argued marketing is a battle for a place in the customer's mind, not the marketplace.
The specific group of people a product is designed and marketed for. Knowing exactly who you're selling to sharpens every other decision.
Their target market was busy urban parents, so every ad spoke to convenience and time saved.
A straightforward compound — the 'market' you 'target'. Trying to appeal to everyone usually means resonating with no one.
Unique Selling Proposition — the one distinct benefit that sets a product apart from every competitor. The single reason to choose you over them.
Their USP was a lifetime warranty no rival offered, the anchor of every pitch and ad.
Coined by advertising pioneer Rosser Reeves in the 1940s–50s — the unique claim a product can make that competitors can't or don't.
Dividing a broad market into smaller groups with shared needs or traits, so marketing can be tailored to each. Different messages for different people.
Through segmentation, they split customers into bargain-hunters and luxury buyers, marketing to each differently.
From 'segment' (a part cut off). Rather than one message for all, the market is sliced into groups that each respond to different appeals.
The classic marketing mix: Product, Price, Place, and Promotion — the four levers a business controls to market effectively. A foundational framework.
They reviewed the Four Ps: refining the product, adjusting price, expanding distribution, and boosting promotion.
Formalized by marketing professor E. Jerome McCarthy in 1960, the alliterative 'Four Ps' became the most-taught framework in all of marketing.
Value Proposition — a clear statement of the benefit a customer gets, why it matters, and why it's better than alternatives. The promise that earns the sale.
Their value proposition was simple: save two hours a week for the price of a coffee.
A compound of 'value' (worth) and 'proposition' (a proposal). It answers the customer's core question: 'why should I care, and why you?'
Buyer Persona — a detailed, semi-fictional profile of an ideal customer, with goals, frustrations, and behaviors. A tool for keeping real people in focus.
They built a persona named 'Busy Brenda', a time-strapped manager, and wrote every email as if speaking to her.
From Latin 'persona' (a theatrical mask, a character). Marketers craft these 'characters' to humanize abstract market segments into someone you can picture.
Search Engine Optimization — the practice of improving a website so it ranks higher in search results like Google, driving free 'organic' traffic.
Strong SEO put their blog on the first page of Google, bringing thousands of visitors without ad spend.
An acronym from the late 1990s as businesses raced to rank in early search engines. The whole industry exists to court the algorithms' favor.
Pay-Per-Click — an advertising model where you pay only when someone clicks your ad. The engine behind search and social ads.
Their PPC campaign cost two dollars per click but each click was a genuinely interested buyer.
Descriptive of the pricing: you pay 'per click', not per view. It tied ad cost directly to measurable action — a revolution in advertising accountability.
Attracting customers by creating valuable, relevant content (articles, videos, guides) rather than directly pitching products. Earning attention instead of buying it.
Instead of ads, they used content marketing — a helpful blog that drew readers who became loyal customers.
A modern formalization of an old idea (a tractor company's farming magazine dates to 1895); the term captures marketing through usefulness rather than interruption.
Call to Action — a prompt telling the audience exactly what to do next: 'Buy Now', 'Sign Up', 'Learn More'. The nudge that converts interest into action.
Changing the CTA from 'Submit' to 'Get My Free Guide' doubled the signups overnight.
Descriptive: it 'calls' the audience to 'action'. The clearer and more compelling the instruction, the more people follow it.
'Organic' reach is unpaid — earned through search, sharing, and content; 'paid' reach is bought through advertising. Most strategies blend both.
Their organic posts built a loyal base slowly, while paid ads gave instant reach for the product launch.
'Organic' from the sense of natural, unforced growth (like an organism); 'paid' is its blunt opposite — reach you purchase rather than earn.
Comparing two versions of something (an ad, page, or email) by showing each to different audiences to see which performs better. Letting data decide.
A/B testing two subject lines revealed the question-style one got 30% more opens.
From the simple labeling of version 'A' versus version 'B'. The method borrows the logic of controlled experiments and brings it to marketing decisions.
Promoting products and nurturing relationships through email. Despite being one of the oldest digital channels, it remains among the most profitable.
Their weekly email newsletter quietly drove more sales than every social platform combined.
Born with email itself; the first marketing email was reportedly sent in 1978. Decades on, its measurable, direct-to-inbox reach keeps it a workhorse channel.
Influencer Marketing — partnering with people who have engaged online audiences to promote products to their followers, borrowing their trust and reach.
A single post from the right influencer sold out their entire inventory within hours.
From 'influence' — these creators 'influence' their audience's choices. The practice exploded with social media, turning personal reach into marketing currency.
The sales funnel — the journey customers take from first awareness to final purchase, visualized as a funnel because many enter the top and few reach the bottom.
They mapped the funnel and found most prospects dropped off at checkout, so they fixed that step first.
From the funnel's shape: wide at the top (many aware) narrowing to a spout (few buyers). A model of how interest converts to sales, stage by stage.
Conversion Rate — the percentage of people who take a desired action (buy, sign up, click) out of everyone who had the chance. The core measure of effectiveness.
A 5% conversion rate meant 5 of every 100 visitors bought — and small improvements meant big money.
From 'convert' (to change) — a visitor 'converts' into a customer. The whole field of conversion optimization exists to nudge this number up.
Click-Through Rate — the percentage of people who click an ad or link after seeing it. A key gauge of how compelling your message is.
The ad's high CTR proved the headline grabbed attention, even if not everyone bought.
Descriptive: the rate at which viewers 'click through' to the next step. One of the oldest and most-watched digital advertising metrics.
Customer Acquisition Cost — the total cost of marketing and sales divided by the number of new customers gained. What it actually costs to win one buyer.
If CAC was $50 but each customer spent $200, the math worked beautifully.
An acronym central to growth math; paired with LTV, it answers the make-or-break question: do customers earn back more than they cost to acquire?
Lifetime Value — the total revenue a business expects from a single customer over their entire relationship. The number that justifies acquisition spending.
With an LTV of $1,200, they could comfortably spend $200 to acquire each new subscriber.
Also written 'CLV' or 'CLTV'. The principle: a customer's worth isn't one sale but the sum of all future ones — which sets how much you can spend to get them.
Return on Ad Spend — revenue generated for every dollar spent on advertising. The advertising world's version of ROI.
A 4:1 ROAS meant every dollar of ad spend brought back four in sales — a campaign worth scaling.
An acronym narrowing ROI to advertising specifically; it answers the marketer's daily question: are these ads actually paying for themselves?
'Impressions' count how many times content is shown (including repeats to the same person); 'reach' counts how many unique people saw it. Volume versus audience size.
The post earned 50,000 impressions but only 20,000 reach — many people saw it more than once.
'Impression' from the sense of leaving a mark on a viewer; 'reach' from how far the message spreads. Confusing the two is a classic rookie metrics error.
The percentage of visitors who land on a page and leave without doing anything else. A high bounce rate often signals a mismatch between expectation and content.
A 90% bounce rate on the landing page warned them the ad was promising something the page didn't deliver.
From the image of a visitor 'bouncing' straight back off the site like a ball — arriving and immediately leaving without engaging.
A potential customer who has shown some interest — by signing up, downloading, or inquiring. The raw material of the sales process.
The webinar generated 200 leads, names and emails of people curious enough to learn more.
From the sense of a 'lead' as a clue or tip pointing somewhere promising — a thread of interest the sales team can follow toward a sale.
A lead who has been qualified as a genuine potential buyer — they have the need, budget, and authority to purchase. A lead worth pursuing seriously.
After qualifying, half the leads became real prospects worth the sales team's time.
From Latin 'prospectus' (a view, outlook) — a prospect represents a promising 'outlook' for a sale, a step beyond an unvetted lead.
The visual map of all deals in progress, organized by stage from first contact to closing. Lets sales teams forecast revenue and spot bottlenecks.
Her pipeline showed ten deals near closing, giving the team confidence in hitting the quarterly target.
From the image of an oil or water pipeline — deals 'flow' through stages toward the close. A healthy pipeline keeps revenue flowing steadily.
Cold outreach — contacting a potential customer who has had no prior relationship with you. The hardest, most rejection-heavy form of selling.
He made fifty cold calls a day, knowing most would say no but a few would turn into deals.
'Cold' because there's no prior warmth or relationship — you're reaching out from nowhere. Contrasts with a 'warm' lead who already knows you.
Determining whether a lead is actually a good fit worth pursuing — checking their need, budget, authority, and timeline before investing effort.
By qualifying early, she avoided wasting weeks on prospects who could never afford the product.
From 'qualify' (to meet required conditions). The sales discipline of separating real buyers from time-wasters before pouring in effort.
The final stage of a sale — getting the prospect to commit and sign. 'Closing the deal' is the moment everything has been building toward.
After weeks of nurturing, she closed the deal with a limited-time offer that prompted the signature.
From 'close' (to finish or conclude). Immortalized in sales lore by the mantra 'Always Be Closing' — the art of guiding a prospect to yes.
'Upselling' encourages a bigger or premium purchase; 'cross-selling' adds related products. Both increase the value of an existing sale.
At checkout, the upsell to the deluxe version and a cross-sell of accessories raised the average order.
'Upsell' from selling 'up' to a higher tier; 'cross-sell' from selling 'across' into related items. The classic 'would you like fries with that?' is a cross-sell.
To make brief contact to check in or align. A staple of sales and office speak for a quick catch-up conversation.
She promised to touch base next week to see if he was ready to move forward.
From baseball, where a runner must 'touch base' to be safe — borrowed into business as a quick check-in to stay connected and on track.
Rapid, creative, often low-cost experimentation to grow a company fast — especially startups. Unconventional tactics over traditional marketing budgets.
Through clever growth hacking — a referral loop baked into the product — they grew from zero to a million users.
Coined by Sean Ellis in 2010 for a marketer 'whose true north is growth' — using scrappy, data-driven tricks rather than big-budget campaigns.
Content that spreads rapidly and widely as people share it with others, multiplying reach explosively and organically. The marketer's jackpot.
The quirky video went viral, racking up ten million views without a cent of ad spend.
From the medical 'virus' — content spreads person-to-person like an infection, each share sparking more. 'Virality' became marketing's most coveted, least controllable goal.
Content that stays relevant and useful indefinitely, rather than going stale. A guide to 'how to tie a tie' keeps drawing readers for years.
Their evergreen tutorial kept pulling in steady traffic years after it was published.
From evergreen trees that stay green year-round — content that never withers, in contrast to timely, perishable news that quickly dates.
The portion of a webpage visible without scrolling — prime real estate where the most important content and calls to action belong.
They moved the signup button above the fold, and conversions jumped now that no one had to scroll to find it.
Borrowed from newspapers, where the most important stories ran 'above the fold' — visible on the top half of a folded front page on the newsstand.
A 'warm' lead already knows you or has shown real interest; a 'cold' lead has had no contact. Warm leads convert far more easily.
Referrals were warm leads who already trusted them, closing in half the time of cold prospects.
The temperature metaphor: 'warm' from existing rapport and interest, 'cold' from starting with no relationship at all — warmth measures readiness to buy.
